topic: A homeowner learns their house was a former crime scene
raw_output:
[story 1]

Mark dragged his feet across the worn hardwood floor of the old Victorian. The afternoon sun slanted through dusty, grimy windows, illuminating motes dancing in the stagnant air. He slumped onto the faded velvet sofa, letting his head fall back until it rested against the upholstery. "Ugh," he mumbled, the sound thick with profound inertia. He’d inherited the place from a distant relative he barely remembered, and the thought of cleaning anything felt like climbing a mountain. As he idly traced a finger over a faint, almost invisible smudge on the baseboard near the fireplace, a cold prickle ran up his spine. It wasn't just dust; there was something else, a subtle wrongness in the texture of the paint. He stood up, stretched with a groan that seemed to vibrate through his joints, and walked toward the study. The air in that room felt heavy, still, as if time itself had paused. He peered at the antique desk, and there, beneath a layer of undisturbed grime, was a faint discoloration, a shape that made his stomach clench.

[story 2]

Eleanor sighed, a long, drawn-out exhalation that seemed to carry the weight of all her current motivations. She was draped across the armchair, a half-finished novel resting unread on her lap, the remote control dangling forgotten from her fingers. Why move? The thought was utterly exhausting. The house, sprawling and shadowed, offered no immediate challenge, only a vast expanse of things to ignore. She yawned, a wide, unrestrained display of fatigue, and surveyed the room with half-lidded eyes. While idly shifting her weight, her gaze snagged on a peculiar pattern near the hallway rug—a disruption in the usual wear and tear. Curiosity, a sensation she usually reserved for high-stakes situations, flickered weakly. She rose slowly, deliberately, her movements heavy and unhurried. As she approached the spot, the faint outline resolved itself: an anomaly in the floorboards, a place where the surface seemed subtly disturbed, as if something heavy had rested there long ago.

[story 3]

For David, the sheer effort of existing seemed insurmountable. He was sprawled on the floor of the living room, a discarded pizza box serving as a makeshift pillow. The world outside the window was a blur of indifferent activity, and he found the internal resistance to even minor exertion overwhelming. He blinked slowly, his eyelids feeling glued shut. "Whatever," he muttered into the carpet, his voice barely a whisper of sound. He shifted again, a slow, deliberate roll onto his side, seeking the most comfortable angle of repose. It was in this state of near-total physical surrender that he noticed it—a faint, almost imperceptible sheen on the dark wood paneling behind the bookshelf. It wasn't water damage or simple aging. It was a specific kind of residue, a lingering trace that suggested an event had occurred here, a history etched into the very structure of the room, something he certainly didn't have the energy to investigate.
